text.title: Change Control Rules and Anti-Patterns
text.summary: Rules for rejected CR tracking, hidden-change detection, agentic CR workflow, and the failure modes that make change control theatre.

rule.statement: Track rejected CRs with reason codes; the same request appearing three or more times is a real requirements signal that the scope statement missed something important.
rule.rationale: Repeated rejections signal a stakeholder need that the current baseline does not address; ignoring the pattern leads to workarounds, shadow implementations, or post-launch demands that cost more than addressing the need would have.

rule.statement: Review merged PRs weekly against the current scope baseline to detect developer-driven scope additions that bypassed the CR process.
rule.rationale: Gold plating and feature additions that appear in code but not in any CR are invisible to the change register; they consume budget, add untested scope, and may fail acceptance.

antipattern.description: Treating developer-initiated refactors that change observable behavior as out-of-scope for change control.
antipattern.reason: Behavioral changes affect the scope baseline regardless of their technical framing; refactors that add, remove, or alter features must follow the CR process even if the developer considers them "internal" changes.

rule.statement: The CR-drafting agent ingest free-form change requests (Slack, email, meeting notes) and normalizes them into typed CR records with WBS IDs touched and needs_clarification[] fields; humans retain decision authority and the budget veto.
rule.rationale: Agent normalization removes the bottleneck of PM transcription while preserving human judgment on approval; the agent surfaces incomplete information explicitly rather than letting it hide in narrative.

rule.statement: The CR agent must never transition a CR to "Approved" status; only named authority holders approve — the agent drafts, logs, and flags, never decides.
rule.rationale: Automated approval removes human accountability; a CR approved by a bot has no accountable decision-maker and cannot be used as evidence in contract disputes or audits.

antipattern.description: LLM-generated impact analyses that underestimate testing and regression effort by treating "test impact" as a sub-item of "quality" rather than a separate line item.
antipattern.reason: Testing effort for scope changes is typically 30-50% of implementation effort; bundling it into "quality" makes it invisible and leads to systematically undercosted CRs.

rule.statement: Define an emergency-CR fast lane for incident-triggered changes: abbreviated impact analysis (30-minute time-box), single-approver authority, and mandatory retroactive full documentation within 24 hours.
rule.rationale: Without a fast lane, teams bypass change control entirely during incidents to meet response SLAs; the fast lane maintains the audit trail while respecting the urgency.
